benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: 22-Jun official cash $3,405.00 (Westmetall), essentially flat vs 19-Jun $3,400.00. The Mon intraday gap to ~$3,600 on Iran's Sat 20-Jun Hormuz-closure declaration completely unwound by the close as the closure was contested (CENTCOM: 55 ships transited Sat) and a US–Iran communication line was set up at Geneva on 21-Jun. Price now consolidating around $3,400, near a 2-month low, off the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855). avg = May-2026 monthly mean ($3,670.18).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 22-Jun official 3M $3,406.00 vs 19-Jun $3,398.00 (+$8). Live trading back in the ~$3,400 region after the intraday Hormuz spike faded; easing supply risk + firm USD + rising China/Indonesia output cap the front. avg = May-2026 mean ($3,600.79).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: 22-Jun cash $3,405 vs 3M $3,406 = −$1 = slipping into mild CONTANGO (19-Jun was +$2 backwardation). The acute front-end war premium that briefly snapped to backwardation on the reclosure declaration has deflated again as the strait stayed open in practice — the curve is back to roughly flat/very mild contango, consistent with easing near-term scarcity over an intact structural deficit.

benchmark.name: SHFE front-month (excl VAT)

benchmark.name: Alumina API / Platts region

benchmark.name: LME inventory (tonnes)

macro_events: WAR PREMIUM ROARING BACK — HORMUZ RECLOSED, PRICE GAPS UP ~6%. Aluminium opens Mon 22-Jun surging to ~$3,614 cash / ~$3,604 3M (Trading Economics live; open $3,673, intraday range $3,587–3,676) after Iran's armed forces declared the Strait of Hormuz CLOSED again on Sat 20-Jun — just three days after it reopened — blaming Israel for continued strikes in southern Lebanon and the US for failing to uphold the ceasefire. Latest confirmed Westmetall official is Fri 19-Jun (cash $3,400.00 / 3M $3,398.00); Monday officials print ~PM London.

Conflicting closure claims: Iran says the Strait is shut 'in response to the continuous violation of the ceasefire'; the US disputes it — VP JD Vance said 'the straits really are open' and CENTCOM reported transit actually INCREASED Saturday (55 merchant ships, 17M+ bbl of oil). But CNBC/independent tracking showed weekend tanker transits falling (~12 Sun vs 21+ Sat), and shipowners are again hesitating. VP Vance arrived in Switzerland Sun for high-level talks with Iran to clarify ceasefire terms.

Physical reality unchanged and now re-threatened: the Gulf supplies ~9–10% of global aluminium (~25% of ex-China) with ~3–3.2 Mt of capacity crippled (EGA Al Taweelah ~12-mo restore; ALBA partially suspended). The brief 3-day reopening had begun to normalise logistics; the reclosure declaration re-imperils the Gulf export corridor and the smelter-restart timeline, reviving the acute-scarcity narrative.

Macro still a partial offset: the June FOMC's hawkish lean keeps the USD firm (DXY ~99.9) and is disinflationary, but the renewed escalation re-injects a geopolitical risk premium across the complex (gold ~$4,344, Brent ~$78–79). Analysts (Citi/Goldman) had set near-term targets at ~$3,600 with upside toward $4,000 if the Strait stays shut beyond ~30 days.

Ex-China supply pressure persists as the counterweight: China unwrought + semis exports ran +15.5% YoY to 632kt in May (Fastmarkets sees 2026 China exports +12–18%, potentially ~7.2 Mt) and Indonesian smelter output is rising — the structural bearish offset to the renewed war premium. The cash–3M curve has snapped back to backwardation (~+$10).
lme_commentary: Commentary: the 22-Jun Westmetall official confirmed cash $3,405.00 / 3M $3,406.00 (stock 313,800 t, −1,500 t) — essentially flat vs 19-Jun ($3,400 / $3,398) and a full round-trip from Monday's intraday ~6% gap to ~$3,600 on Iran's Hormuz-closure declaration. The reclosure was contested (CENTCOM: 55 ships Saturday) and a US–Iran communication line was set up at Geneva on 21-Jun, so the brief war premium deflated and the cash–3M curve slipped to mild contango (−$1) from +$2. Price is consolidating around $3,400 near a 2-month low, off the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855) and above the 16-Jun cycle low ($3,359). The structural deficit is intact (EGA ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial, LME stock 313,800 t drawing) and keeps a floor, but with the acute Gulf catalyst fading the tape lacks a fresh upside driver. Headline risk stays two-sided on the reclosure's durability vs the US dispute.

alumina_notes: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$352/t) softer on Q2 oversupply vs Platts API region (~$305/t); a ~1.2 Mt 2026 surplus is building on higher production/shipments into subdued demand.
Hormuz reclosure (Sat 20-Jun) re-introduces a Gulf alumina-logistics risk — ~60% of regional alumina imports transit the Strait; the brief reopening's flow-normalization is now in question.

Bauxite: Guinea's oversupply pushed CIF China to ~$66/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$34); Guinea is weighing export curbs (a >$100/dmt target) — a structural upside risk to raw-material cost independent of the Gulf.
Refining: Gulf gas-supply risk re-emerges with the renewed escalation; EGA's Al Taweelah refinery restart timeline is again uncertain.
Net: alumina balance still skews to surplus (ample supply caps the price), but the reclosure adds a logistics/energy upside risk and Guinea bauxite-curb risk keeps a floor under raw-material cost.
net_read: DE-ESCALATION — THE WAR-PREMIUM SPIKE FADES, PRICE CONSOLIDATES NEAR A 2-MONTH LOW. Aluminium's run to a four-year high ($3,855 cash, 02-Jun) was built on the Gulf supply shock (EGA Al Taweelah and ALBA outages, Strait-of-Hormuz disruption, ~3 Mt removed). Iran's Sat 20-Jun declaration that Hormuz was CLOSED again briefly re-inflated the war premium — aluminium gapped up ~6% intraday Monday to ~$3,600 — but that spike fully unwound into the close: the 22-Jun official settled flat at cash $3,405.00 / 3M $3,406.00 (Westmetall), and the cash–3M curve slipped to mild contango (−$1). The closure proved contested (CENTCOM reported 55 ships transited Saturday) and a US–Iran communication line was established at Geneva on 21-Jun, so the strait is open in practice. Price is now consolidating around $3,400, near a 2-month low, with easing supply risk, a firm dollar (Fed hawkish hold), and rising China/Indonesia output capping rallies. The structural-deficit floor stays intact — EGA ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial, LME stock drawing to 313,800 t (~92% Russian-origin) — but with the acute logistics shock fading, the bullish-supply narrative has lost its near-term catalyst. US physical premiums stay tariff-inflated under the 50% Section 232 rate. Headline risk remains two-sided: a credible re-closure would re-ignite the premium, while durable de-escalation argues for a drift back toward the $3,260–$3,080 base.

feed.text: Hormuz transit sputtered: only ~12 ships transited Sunday (vs ~21 Sat); 5 of 8 inbound vessels went 'dark' (AIS off) after Iran's renewed closure declaration — corridor flow re-impaired even as CENTCOM/Washington maintain the strait is legally open (60-day MoU transit window).

Westmetall confirmed 22-Jun LME officials flat: cash $3,405.00 / 3M $3,406.00; stock 313,800 t (−1,500 t). Monday's intraday ~6% gap to ~$3,600 on the Hormuz-closure declaration fully unwound into the close; cash–3M slipped to mild contango (−$1).

US and Iran established a 'communication line' at Geneva to avoid incidents and ensure safe passage for commercial vessels during a 60-day window — the closure functioned as symbolic leverage rather than a physical shutdown. War premium deflates.
Iran's armed forces declared the Strait of Hormuz CLOSED, citing Israeli ceasefire violations; the brief catalyst behind Monday's intraday spike — but the move faded as the closure was disputed.
Closure contested: CENTCOM reported transit INCREASED Saturday — 55 merchant ships, 17M+ bbl — and VP Vance said 'the straits really are open.' The strait stayed open in practice.
Brent eased back to ~$76 (from ~$79) and gold softened to ~$4,330 as the Gulf risk premium deflated; DXY firm near 99.95 on the FOMC hawkish hold.
Gulf capacity still crippled — EGA Al Taweelah ~12 months to restore, ALBA partially suspended; LME stock 313,800 t and drawing. The deficit floor is intact even as the acute corridor risk fades.
Weak China data plus rising China and Indonesia smelter output add downside pressure as the war premium unwinds.
Q3 MJP negotiations open with the de-escalation skewing trader expectations back down toward ~$285–340/t (from spike-driven $340–360).

news.headline: 22-Jun LME official flat at cash $3,405.00 / 3M $3,406.00 (Westmetall); Monday's intraday ~6% spike to ~$3,600 on the Hormuz reclosure fully faded. Cash–3M slipped to mild contango (−$1); price consolidating near a 2-month low ~$3,400.

Iran redeclared Hormuz closed on Sat 20-Jun; the US disputed it (CENTCOM: 55 ships Sat), but weekend transit thinned to ~12 ships Sunday (from ~21 Sat) with inbound vessels going dark — the corridor is sputtering despite the 60-day MoU window.
news.source: CNBC / CENTCOM / Al Jazeera / NBC News (22-Jun-2026)

news.headline: Capacity still crippled: Middle East ~9–10% of global supply (~25% ex-China), ~3 Mt hit by the 28-Mar EGA/ALBA strikes. EGA Al Taweelah ~12 months to restore; ALBA partial — structural deficit intact, providing the floor.
Brent eased to ~$76 and gold to ~$4,330 as the Gulf risk premium deflated; DXY firm near 99.95 on the FOMC hawkish hold — a headwind for $-priced metals.

Citi/Goldman near-term ~$3,600 with upside toward $4,000 ONLY if the Strait stays shut beyond ~30 days — a scenario the de-escalation has pushed back. Demand concerns and rising China/Indonesia output cited as downside.
Cash–3M curve slipped to mild contango (−$1) from +$2 as front-end scarcity eased; total stocks stay low (~313.8 kt) and drawing.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 22-Jun (no new releases). Q2 season begins mid/late-July: Alcoa confirmed 22-Jul (after close); Norsk Hydro late-Jul (has hedged ~67% of 2026 primary output at ~$3,000/t and ~51% of premiums at ~$571/t, net realized premium guided $530–580/t). Sources: company releases & SEC/exchange filings, Alcircle, Discovery Alert, Investing.com, Zawya/TradeArabia, GuruFocus.

commercial.text: The Hormuz reclosure has re-ignited the rally — price gapped up ~6% to ~$3,614 cash / ~$3,604 3M and the curve is back in backwardation. Capture the move: accelerate near-dated realization on unpriced tonnes and ensure LME hedges are in place on priced volume, but do NOT over-hedge forward into a contested, headline-driven spike that could reverse if the US dispute (CENTCOM: traffic continues) prevails. As a non-disrupted supplier, prioritise value-added (billet/slab/PFA) premium capture while the supply scare is hot.

The renewed Gulf risk reverses last week's premium easing: CIF Japan spot is re-firming (~$361) and Q3 MJP negotiations now skew back up to ~$290–360 (vs the $250–350 of the brief reopening). Lock elevated premiums on renewals and spot allocations NOW while EGA/ALBA remain impaired and the export corridor is re-threatened. US MW stays structurally elevated on the 50% Section 232 tariff.

Ex-China FOB Australia (~$352/t) vs softer API region (~$305/t); the Q2 surplus caps it but the reclosure re-introduces Gulf alumina-logistics risk. If net seller, lock firm ex-China pricing into the renewed strength; if buyer for smelting, the surplus still offers softer windows — but transact ahead of any logistics re-disruption.

Reverse course from last week's normalization: with Iran declaring Hormuz closed again, REINSTATE contingency routing, keep war-risk cover live and demurrage clauses active. The closure is contested (US says traffic continues), so plan for two-sided disruption — secure vessel capacity and alternative routing (land-bridge to Jeddah/Red Sea) now rather than assuming the brief reopening holds.

Manage the whipsaw with strategic accounts: the market has flipped from premium-unwind back to supply-scare within days. Reaffirm reliability and OTIF, keep allocation transparent through the volatility, and convert dependability into premium tolerance and relationship equity.

Track: (1) the DURABILITY of the reclosure vs the US dispute — actual Hormuz transit counts, CENTCOM vs Iran claims, the Switzerland talks; (2) whether price holds the gap (~$3,600) or fades toward $3,400 if traffic continues; (3) EGA/ALBA restart timelines; (4) USD/Fed path after the hawkish June FOMC; (5) China export pace (Fastmarkets +12–18%), Indonesian ramp & Section 232 (50%); (6) Guinea bauxite-curb risk. Keep allocations flexible — headline risk is acute and two-sided.

bottom_line: A de-escalation, not a breakout. Monday's ~6% intraday spike on Iran's 20-Jun Hormuz-closure declaration fully faded: the 22-Jun official settled flat at cash $3,405.00 / 3M $3,406.00, the cash–3M curve slipped to mild contango (−$1), and price is consolidating near a 2-month low around $3,400. The closure proved contested (CENTCOM: 55 ships Saturday) and a US–Iran communication line was established at Geneva on 21-Jun, so the acute war premium has unwound. The structural deficit (EGA ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial, ~92%-Russian LME stock drawing to 313,800 t) still provides a floor, and Citi/Goldman upside scenarios toward $4,000 remain contingent on a durable re-closure (Strait shut >30 days) that has not materialised. US physical premiums stay tariff-inflated under the 50% Section 232 rate. Net: supply-scare catalyst has faded, base case drifts toward the $3,260–$3,080 confluence unless the Gulf re-escalates — verify the reclosure's durability before fading or chasing.

caveats: Report run Mon 22-Jun-2026. The Monday LME session is live and sharply higher; Monday officials print ~PM London and were not yet posted at run time. Benchmark/KPI 'current' values are the Monday intraday live read (~$3,614 cash / ~$3,604 3M, Trading Economics); 'previous' and the price chart use the latest confirmed Fri 19-Jun officials (cash $3,400.00 / 3M $3,398.00 / stock 315,300 t).

The ~6% Monday gap-up is driven by Iran's Sat 20-Jun declaration that the Strait of Hormuz is closed again. This claim is CONTESTED — the US (VP Vance, CENTCOM) says traffic continues (55 ships Saturday). The move is headline-driven and two-sided; a credible, durable reopening could fade it back toward ~$3,400.
LME stock figure (315,300 t) is the 19-Jun official; Monday's stock report was pending at run time — re-validate at the Monday update.
Macro/FX/energy values are latest point-in-time reads — oil (Brent ~$78–79 / WTI ~$75), DXY (~99.9) and gold (~$4,344); re-validate against live feeds intraday.

Daily physical premium assessments (MJP, CIF Japan, EDU, Rotterdam billets, US Midwest) sit behind PRA paywalls (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus). Values shown are the most recent public anchors / status for the 2026 war-elevated regime — reconcile to the desk's licensed feed before pricing.
SHFE front-month is a last-reported reference (~CNY 24,400/t SMM spot, excl-VAT USD equivalent); verify on SMM / SHFE for the live print.
Alumina indices diverge by methodology (Fastmarkets/SMM FOB Australia vs Platts API region) — use the index contractually relevant to the trade.
This report is market intelligence, not trading or investment advice. Figures are point-in-time and should be re-validated before commercial commitments.

Analyst consensus, raised; Citi/Goldman ~$3,600 near-term with $4,000 upside if Strait stays shut >30 days

outlook.curve_note: Front-end backwardation, which had deflated to ~flat, snapped back to a ~+$10 cash–3M premium on the Hormuz reclosure — acute near-term scarcity re-priced. Consensus 2026 average lifts toward the $3,300–3,650 band before easing into 2027 as Gulf supply is eventually repaired. The contested nature of the closure keeps the curve headline-sensitive.

ai_analysis: What changed: the war-premium unwind has violently reversed. After basing ~$3,400 last week on the brief US-Iran MOU, Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz closed again on Sat 20-Jun (3 days after reopening), and aluminium gapped up ~6% Monday to ~$3,614 cash / ~$3,604 3M (open $3,673, range $3,587–3,676) — the supply-shock narrative is back in control.
Term structure: the cash–3M curve snapped back to backwardation (cash ~+$10 vs 3M) from ~flat as the reclosure revived acute front-end scarcity; total LME stocks keep drawing (315,300 t, −38% YTD).

The key caveat — contested closure: the US disputes Iran's claim (VP Vance: 'the straits really are open'; CENTCOM: transit increased Sat, 55 ships). Independent tracking showed weekend transits falling and owners hesitating. The spike is headline-driven and two-sided; a credible, durable reopening could fade it back toward $3,400.

Macro a partial offset: the June FOMC's hawkish lean keeps the USD firm (DXY ~99.9); but the renewed escalation re-injects a risk premium (gold ~$4,344, Brent ~$78–79). Citi/Goldman upside scenarios toward $4,000 (if the Strait stays shut >30 days) are back in play.

Forward bias: base case (45%) = elevated but choppy ($3,400–$3,700) as the closure proves contested; bull case raised to 40% (reclosure holds/escalates → $3,800–4,100+); bear 15% (durable reopening fades it). Near-term watch is Hormuz transit counts and the Switzerland talks; main upside = a sustained closure + slipped EGA/ALBA restart, main downside = the US dispute prevailing.

premium_explainer.desc: Quarterly-negotiated premium over LME cash for primary metal delivered to Japan — the Asian benchmark.
Spot premium for cargoes delivered cost-insurance-freight to Japan; leads the next MJP negotiation.
Premium for non-EU-origin metal in-warehouse Rotterdam before the 3% import duty — the European spot gauge.
Value-added premium for extrusion billet (conversion + logistics) on top of the ingot premium.
Delivered-Midwest premium over LME; heavily driven by US Section 232 tariffs and import economics.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): MJP Q2 settled near an 11-yr high (~$351.5/t, +80% q/q); CIF Japan spot easing (~$355) as the Hormuz reclosure proved contested and unwound. Q3 negotiations open with the de-escalation skewing the range toward ~$285–340 (Platts/Fastmarkets).
European (EDU): ~$483/t, softening vs last month (~$519 avg) on weak demand but underpinned by the 6% duty + CBAM.
US Midwest: MW US Transaction ~$2,532/t; duty-paid P1020A ~$2,158/t — structurally elevated on the 50% Section 232 tariff, easing only marginally with the LME.

Common thread: premiums spiked on the Gulf outage, briefly re-firmed on the 20-Jun reclosure declaration, and are now easing again as the strait stays open in practice — unrepaired Gulf capacity keeps a floor while the policy (tariff/CBAM) overlay holds US/EU premiums structurally high.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$66 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$34); oversupply has roughly halved prices since Jan-25 — Guinea weighing export curbs toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Fastmarkets/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$352/t) easing on Q2 oversupply; API region softer (~$305; FOB futures ~$307). Surplus building into H2, but the Hormuz reclosure adds a Gulf-logistics risk.
Bauxite: Guinea oversupply pushed CIF China to ~$66/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$34) — Guinea's export-curb threat (a >$100/dmt target) is the key upside risk to raw-material cost.
Caustic soda firm (~$500/t FOB NE Asia) — a sticky refining cost; lime stable. Net refining-cost pressure eases with alumina/bauxite but caustic and renewed Gulf energy risk keep a floor.
Read-through: softer alumina + bauxite lower a smelter's input cost; for an alumina seller, oversupply caps realizations into Q3 even as the reclosure threatens Gulf flows.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen; rising carbon-cost exposure

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-traded — full transparency (3M ~$13,595/t, ~$6.34/lb, steadied on strong China data; COMEX near records on US tariff premium); alloy input for 2xxx & some 6xxx

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Bulk-freight proxy for bauxite/alumina; ~2,600 (19-Jun), near a multi-month low; Capesize BCI ~3,800 — Trading Economics / HandyBulk / Bloomberg

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.note: ~80–85% of Gulf Al exports & ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz. After Iran's Sat 20-Jun reclosure declaration, weekend transit thinned sharply — only ~12 ships Sunday vs ~21 Saturday, with most inbound vessels going 'dark' (AIS off). The US disputes the closure (CENTCOM: 55 ships Sat; VP Vance: 'the straits really are open') and a 60-day charge-free MoU transit window is nominally in force, but in practice the corridor is re-impaired and owners are again hesitating. Demining due within 30 days of the 17-Jun MoU.

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: Surcharges/war-risk premia re-firming on the reclosure declaration after last week's brief easing; keep cover live through the volatility and the 60-day ceasefire window.

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: Trucking workaround — reinstate as a contingency given the renewed Hormuz risk; costly but de-risks the corridor.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: Re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure and keep demurrage clauses live until routing normalises

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (largest cost — watch PPAs/carbon), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency — the biggest carbon risk), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, ~$13,600/t).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China) — candidates to diversify or contract forward.

Logistics is the swing factor — and it just flipped back to RISK: Iran's Hormuz reclosure declaration reverses last week's freight/war-risk easing. Reinstate contingency routing and keep cover live; BDI near a 2026-cycle low (~2,600) still eases bulk costs.

Procurement read: power + carbon (coke/pitch) drive roughly half of conversion cost — prioritise these for hedging / forward contracting; alloying metals are smaller line items but China-concentrated.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) topped at the $3,855 four-year high (early Jun-2026) — a Hormuz-driven fifth-wave blow-off confirmed by bearish weekly RSI divergence. Wave A bottomed at the 16-Jun $3,359 cycle low; wave B rallied to test the .50 retracement (~$3,607 intraday, 22-Jun) on Iran's Hormuz-reclosure declaration but FAILED to hold — officials settled back at $3,405 as the closure was contested. The failure at the .50 weakens the bull alternate (that $3,855 was wave 3 with a fifth wave extending) and reinstates the base case as primary. Base case (~50%): wave B is complete or near-complete in the $3,400–$3,607 band, with wave C to resume toward the $3,260–$3,080 confluence (the prior 4th-of-lesser-degree base / .382–.50 retrace of the whole advance). Bull alternate ~35%, valid above ~$3,000 and confirmed only on a weekly close above $3,666 (.618) / $3,855; deeper bear extension below ~$2,894 ~15%. The bearish-primary count is invalidated on a sustained weekly close above $3,855. Medium confidence; no invalidation breached, but the faded spike has tilted near-term risk back to the downside.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Short-term (daily): the decline off $3,855 unfolded as wave A into the 16-Jun $3,359 low; wave B bounced to ~$3,400, then spiked ~6% intraday to ~$3,607 on Mon 22-Jun (Hormuz reclosure) — exactly the .50 retracement of the A-wave drop — and was REJECTED, closing back at $3,405. That .50 failure argues wave B has topped and wave C lower is underway, targeting the $3,320–$3,260 zone first and the $3,080 confluence on extension. Alternation holds: A was a sharp 5-wave decline, so B ran as a sharp, news-driven retrace rather than a shallow flat. Bullish re-trigger: a daily close > $3,666 (.618) would reopen the $3,855 retest (bull alternate). Bearish-floor invalidation: a sustained break under $3,260 targets $3,150/$3,080. Confidence: moderate — the contested-closure headline risk can still whip the count, but price action (spike sold) favours the bears near-term.

premium_drivers.d: Gulf regional production remains curtailed (EGA/ALBA), but the acute corridor risk has eased: Iran's Sat 20-Jun Hormuz-closure declaration was contested (CENTCOM: 55 ships transited Sat) and a US–Iran communication line was established at Geneva on 21-Jun — the strait is open in practice, so the brief freight/premium spike is unwinding.
50% tariff on full customs value (eff. 8-Jun-2026, through 2027) → US Midwest premiums remain structurally elevated (~98c/lb duty-paid) even as the LME eases.
EU import duty reverted to 6% (from 4%, 1-Jan-2026) plus CBAM → European premiums structurally supported despite soft demand.
Gulf war-risk surcharges and freight easing back as transit continues and the closure is disputed; owners trimming the spike in cover taken after the 20-Jun declaration.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
